stories.headline: Colorado Supreme Court orders Children's Hospital to resume gender-affirming care for minors
stories.summary:
The Colorado Supreme Court on Monday effectively ordered Children's Hospital Colorado to restart gender-affirming care for transgender youth, reversing a lower court's decision. The hospital had paused offering puberty blockers and hormonal treatments in January after the Trump administration threatened to cut federal funding to facilities providing such care to minors.

Families of four transgender children sued, arguing Children's discriminated on the basis of gender identity because it continued offering the same treatments to cisgender children with conditions such as precocious puberty. Denver District Court Judge Ericka Englert had declined to require the hospital to restart care, finding potential harms to the hospital and its broader patient population outweighed harm to plaintiffs. Five of the seven Supreme Court justices disagreed, ruling the lower court erred by allowing discrimination against one group due to potential harm to a larger group.

The Supreme Court sent the case back to the original court with direction to issue an injunction preventing Children's from halting gender-affirming care. Justices Brian Boatwright and Carlos Samour Jr. dissented. In 2025, Children's treated 257 children with puberty blockers and 549 with hormones for gender dysphoria.

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser said hospitals should feel safe providing gender-affirming care in light of the ruling and a separate April decision from an Oregon federal judge. 'I hope the hospital will not delay another day,' Weiser said. A spokesperson for Children's Hospital Colorado said the institution was examining the ruling and could not yet comment.

stories.headline: More than 300 Colorado Democrats seek to censure Gov. Polis over Tina Peters clemency
stories.summary:
More than 300 Colorado Democrats, including elected officials, candidates and organizers, have signed a formal complaint with the state party over Gov. Jared Polis granting clemency to convicted election denier Tina Peters. Peters, the former Mesa County Clerk who was convicted of crimes related to a 2020 voting system breach, is scheduled to be released on parole June 1 under Polis's order.

Signers of the complaint include state Reps. Brianna Titone and Elizabeth Velasco, state Sens. Lisa Cutter and Chris Kolker, along with former lawmakers and county leaders. State Rep. Titone characterized the commutation — announced two days after the legislative session ended — as a strategic move. One signatory, referred to as Kiros, called the decision grounds for impeachment, arguing Polis used a constitutional power to assist an unlawful federal coercion scheme.

Colorado Secretary of State Jena Griswold called Polis's decision 'an affront to our democracy, the people of Colorado, and election officials across the country.' Polis, in an interview with CPR News, said Peters's sentence 'stands out' compared to other nonviolent, first-time offenders.

Convening a special legislative session to pursue impeachment would require either a governor's order or a two-thirds vote of the Legislature. Spokespeople for the Senate president and House speaker did not respond to requests for comment on calls for impeachment, and a Polis spokesperson also did not immediately reply.

stories.headline: Colorado's 2026 legislative session delivers broad wins for business community
stories.summary:
The 2026 Colorado legislative session, which adjourned early Wednesday, proved to be an unusually favorable one for the state's business community, according to a Denver Gazette analysis. Nearly every bill strenuously opposed by business groups was killed or scaled back, and several major victories came in the session's final weeks.

The most widely praised measure was Senate Bill 189, a comprehensive overhaul of Colorado's AI regulations that replaced a 2021 law widely considered unworkable. After three previous reform attempts failed, Gov. Jared Polis convened a closed-door working group that produced a framework mandating consumer disclosure when AI is used in consequential decisions, allowing appeals and data corrections, and giving the state Attorney General enforcement authority. Polis signed SB 189 into law shortly after the session ended.

Business groups also advanced proposals leading to regulatory reform and a streamlined workforce-development system. Two measures that did pass face potential vetoes. Colorado Chamber of Commerce President Loren Furman said the session marked the beginning of a trajectory toward improved national rankings on regulation and cost of living.

'I think it's going to take a couple of years to start seeing some improvements in our rankings on a national basis, but this is a start,' Furman said. Polis, who is term-limited and will leave office in January 2027, described the AI bill as 'a big step in the right direction for Colorado, and a model for the rest of the country.'

stories.headline: Armed man shot and killed by Douglas County deputy after assaulting grandmother, pointing gun at officers
stories.summary:
An armed man was shot and killed inside a Franktown home Sunday afternoon after a Douglas County deputy opened fire during a standoff, according to the Douglas County Sheriff's Office. The incident began when a child called 911 to report that a man was assaulting a grandmother at the residence.

As deputies arrived, a woman ran out of the home. Moments later, a man appeared in the doorway holding a handgun and pointed it at deputies, according to Sheriff Darren Weekly. One deputy fired, striking the man. Investigators have not yet determined whether the fatal wound was caused by the deputy's round or the man's own firearm.

No law enforcement officers were injured in the shooting. The identity of the deceased has not been released pending notification of next of kin. The investigation is ongoing.

Separately, a Douglas County Regional SWAT BearCat responding to the same call crashed in heavy rain on Crowfoot Valley Road. The deputy driving the armored vehicle was injured but is expected to recover; no other occupants were hurt.

stories.headline: Update: Fatal I-25 crash in Douglas County involved driver backing up on highway shoulder, state patrol says
stories.summary:
Investigators have determined that the driver killed Friday in a crash on Interstate 25 near Happy Canyon Road in Douglas County was backing up his Chevrolet Equinox on the right northbound shoulder when an Audi Q5 struck the rear of his vehicle, the Colorado State Patrol said Monday. The Equinox had drifted into the right travel lane as it reversed, and a Toyota Highlander then struck the Audi.

The Equinox driver, a 31-year-old Aurora man, died at the scene. The Douglas County Coroner's Office will release his identity once family notifications are complete. The driver of the Audi, a 46-year-old Parker woman, was hospitalized with serious injuries. The Highlander's driver, a 58-year-old Castle Pines woman, was taken to a hospital with moderate injuries.

The northbound lanes of I-25 were closed for approximately four hours following the crash. The Colorado State Patrol is leading the investigation and is asking any witnesses who have not yet spoken with investigators to call CSP dispatch at 303-239-4501.

stories.headline: Update: Extreme fire weather threatens Sharpe Fire containment in Baca County as southern Colorado braces for conditions
stories.summary:
Fire lines around the Sharpe Fire in Baca County held overnight Sunday into Monday morning, but officials warned that extreme fire weather conditions were expected across southern Colorado throughout Monday, according to Baca County Emergency Management. The National Weather Service issued a warning covering much of the region.

The fire, which originated in Oklahoma and crossed into Colorado, prompted the evacuation of the town of Campo and forced closure of a 77-mile stretch of U.S. 287 from the Oklahoma state line south of Campo to U.S. 50 in Lamar, according to the Colorado Department of Transportation. Gov. Polis's emergency declaration activated the State Emergency Operations Plan and directed the Department of Public Safety to lead response, recovery and mitigation efforts.

The return of dangerous fire weather Monday posed the greatest threat to containment efforts that crews had achieved through the weekend. Authorities continued to urge residents in the evacuation zone to stay out of the area.

stories.headline: Small earthquakes rattle southern Colorado near Walsenburg
stories.summary:
Two small earthquakes were recorded near Walsenburg in southern Colorado over the weekend and early Monday morning, according to the U.S. Geological Survey. A 3.0-magnitude quake struck roughly three miles southwest of Walsenburg just before 6:30 a.m. Monday, followed by the USGS categorizing it as 'weak' — a level that does not typically cause significant damage.

A second, smaller earthquake measuring 2.3 in magnitude was recorded approximately four miles southwest of Walsenburg late Sunday night at about 11:45 p.m. A field manager with the Colorado Division of Homeland Security and Emergency Management and local authorities were investigating Monday to determine whether either earthquake caused any community impact.

stories.headline: Denver's Cherry Creek neighborhood boasts low vacancy rates but faces mounting construction disruptions
stories.summary:
Cherry Creek is navigating an unusual combination of strength and strain, with one of the country's tightest office markets — a 1.9% vacancy rate in Cherry Creek North — even as eight large construction projects currently underway and eight more on the way create significant congestion and access challenges, according to the Cherry Creek Alliance's State of Cherry Creek Report.

The neighborhood generated $119 million in tax revenues for Denver last year, up 3.9% from 2024, and attracted 16.8 million visitors. It now accounts for roughly $1 of every $20 in retail spending citywide. Those figures stand in sharp contrast to downtown Denver, where office vacancy rates approach 40% and some prominent towers are selling at discounts of 90% or more.

Parking has tightened as blocks are redeveloped, and transit access remains a challenge for service and retail workers who largely cannot afford to live nearby. A planned Colorado Boulevard Bus Rapid Transit corridor could eventually ease commutes, but the Glendale City Council voted this month for a 'no build' option through its city limits, complicating the project's scope.

Cherry Creek Alliance CEO Nick LeMasters acknowledged the workforce access problem, noting that employees in hospitality and retail 'by and large do not live in or near Cherry Creek.' The neighborhood's transformation is expected to continue through 2028, with projects representing a mix of office, retail and residential uses.

stories.headline: Speed Bay Warehouse Solutions pays $74 million for 13-building Commerce City industrial complex
stories.summary:
Speed Bay Warehouse Solutions has acquired the Park Industrial Center, a cluster of 13 buildings in Commerce City, for $74 million, according to the Denver Business Journal. Denver-based Kew Realty Corp. was the seller in the transaction.

The deal represents one of the larger industrial real estate transactions in the Denver metro area this year, reflecting continued demand for warehouse and distribution space in the region. Commerce City has been a focal point for logistics and industrial investment given its proximity to Denver International Airport and major highway corridors.

stories.headline: Denver restaurant service charges remain a source of confusion and controversy among diners and workers
stories.summary:
Years after service charges were introduced at Denver restaurants during the COVID-19 pandemic as a wage stabilization tool, the fees continue to generate frustration among customers and employees who find them inconsistent and poorly explained, according to a Denver Post examination. Dozens of Denver restaurants, including major operators such as Culinary Creative Group, Bonanno Concepts and TAG Restaurant Group, continue to levy mandatory charges ranging up to 22%.

The core confusion centers on whether service charges replace tips, supplement them, or fund broader operational costs such as health care premiums and raw materials. Colorado law now requires restaurants to disclose mandatory charges, but customers and workers say compliance has been uneven. Two recent lawsuits filed by restaurant workers over the fees have prompted the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment to announce plans to revisit the issue this summer.

Some restaurant owners say the charges allow them to pay non-tipped back-of-house staff equitably and cover rising costs. Critics, including both diners and servers accustomed to traditional tipping, say the charges are applied inconsistently and that it is often unclear how the money is distributed among staff.

The debate reflects broader pressures on Denver's restaurant industry, which has faced steep inflation and a wave of closures in recent years. The Department of Labor and Employment's review this summer could lead to additional guidance or rule-making on how service charges must be disclosed and distributed.

stories.headline: Rockies defeat Rangers 7-6 at Coors Field in Monday night opener
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies edged the Texas Rangers 7-6 in Monday evening's series opener at Coors Field, according to ESPN's final score report. The game was part of an interleague matchup at Denver's ballpark.

The victory gave the Rockies a measure of rebound after dropping a series finale to the Arizona Diamondbacks 8-6 on Sunday. Outfielder Brenton Doyle was among the featured players for Colorado heading into Monday's contest. The Rangers series continues at Coors Field.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC claims first home victory in Colorado, defeating Orlando Pride 3-1 at DICK'S Sporting Goods Park
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC recorded its first-ever victory on Colorado soil Saturday, defeating the Orlando Pride 3-1 in front of a sellout crowd at DICK'S Sporting Goods Park in Commerce City. The win was the Summit's second consecutive victory and moved the club to 3-3-3 with 12 points on the season.

The result marked a milestone for the expansion club, which had previously achieved its wins away from home. The sellout crowd signaled growing local interest in the team as it establishes itself in the Denver market. Summit FC's next scheduled matches will determine whether the club can sustain its recent momentum.

weather.notable: About 28 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Make no mistake about why these babies are here. They are here to replace us.

topStory.headline: Ukraine Strikes Moscow Region as Trump Halts Iran Attack Plans, Citing Pending Deal
topStory.summary:
Ukraine launched one of its largest drone offensives of the war on Sunday, with Russian authorities reporting their air defenses intercepted 1,054 Ukrainian drones in a single 24-hour period. The attacks killed three people near Moscow, injured 12 others, damaged apartment buildings and private homes, and temporarily forced several Moscow airports to suspend operations. One drone struck the grounds of Sheremetyevo International Airport without causing damage, while another hit an oil storage tank, igniting a blaze visible for miles.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy framed the strikes as direct retaliation for relentless Russian missile and drone attacks on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities in recent days. Moscow Mayor Sergei Sobyanin said 81 drones were intercepted by the capital's air defenses between late Saturday and early Sunday, and a drone hit the territory of a Moscow refinery without halting production. The scale and reach of the Ukrainian strikes made it increasingly difficult for the Kremlin to present the conflict as a distant war with no impact on Russian civilian life.

Separately, President Donald Trump on Monday called off a planned military strike against Iran, citing ongoing negotiations over a potential agreement. In a Truth Social post, Trump directed Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth and senior defense officials to stand down from a scheduled attack but ordered them to remain prepared for a 'full, large scale assault' if an acceptable deal is not reached. Trump said any agreement must include a prohibition on nuclear weapons for Iran.

Iranian officials did not immediately confirm the existence of a pending deal and earlier on Monday stated that their right to enrich uranium is non-negotiable. Sen. Lindsey Graham, a top Trump ally, said any agreement reached between the United States and Iran must come before Congress for approval, drawing a comparison to the Obama-era Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action. Graham outlined U.S. negotiating objectives as including a full halt to uranium enrichment, seizure of Iran's existing nuclear material, and an end to Iranian support for militant groups.

Both situations underscore the volatile state of international security as diplomatic efforts proceed in parallel with active military operations. The Ukraine-Russia war is now in its fifth year, and despite recent statements from both Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin suggesting the conflict may be nearing an end, the pace of strikes on both sides has not slowed. The outcome of Iran negotiations remains uncertain, with a critical deadline implied by Trump's warning and Congress signaling it intends to assert a role in any final agreement.
